name: Demonic Possession
description: Demonic possession is the belief that a supernatural entity, typically considered malevolent (a demon), can inhabit and control a human being's body. This belief is ancient and widespread, appearing in various cultures and religions across the globe, including Christianity, Islam, Judaism, and indigenous spiritual traditions. Proponents of the existence of demonic possession cite anecdotal evidence, personal experiences, and documented cases of individuals exhibiting behaviors such as speaking in unknown languages (xenoglossy), displaying superhuman strength, exhibiting personality changes, aversion to religious symbols, and possessing knowledge they could not have reasonably acquired. They often view these symptoms as evidence of an external, non-physical entity influencing the person's actions and thoughts. Mainstream science and medicine generally reject the concept of demonic possession as a literal phenomenon. They attribute the observed behaviors to psychological disorders such as dissociative identity disorder (DID), schizophrenia, epilepsy, Tourette's syndrome, or other neurological or psychiatric conditions. These perspectives emphasize the importance of medical and psychological evaluation and treatment for individuals exhibiting such symptoms, rather than resorting to religious or spiritual interventions like exorcism. Skeptics also point out that the symptoms of alleged demonic possession can be influenced by cultural expectations, suggestibility, and the power of suggestion. Cases of 'possession' might be understood as manifestations of deep-seated trauma or mental illness expressed through culturally specific frameworks.
subtitle: Inhabitation of a body by a demon

claims.claim: Demons can inhabit and control human bodies.
claims.evidence: Xenoglossy, superhuman strength, personality changes, aversion to religious objects.
claims.criticism: These symptoms can be explained by psychological disorders, neurological conditions, or cultural factors.

claims.claim: Exorcism can successfully remove demons from possessed individuals.
claims.evidence: Anecdotal accounts of successful exorcisms; testimonies from religious figures.
claims.criticism: Placebo effect, suggestibility, misdiagnosis of underlying medical or psychological conditions.

claims.claim: Possession is a genuine spiritual phenomenon and not a mental illness.
claims.evidence: Cases where medical explanations are deemed insufficient; sudden onset of extreme behaviors.
claims.criticism: Difficulty in scientifically verifying the existence of demons; reliance on subjective interpretations of behavior.

keyFigures.name: Richard Gallagher
keyFigures.role: Psychiatrist who claims to have encountered genuine cases of possession

keyFigures.name: Susan Blackmore
keyFigures.role: Parapsychologist and skeptic who argues against the existence of paranormal phenomena

timeline.event: Belief in demons and methods for exorcism
Increased reports of demonic possession and witch hunts
Continued belief in possession within some religious communities; scientific skepticism
Popularization of demonic possession in film and literature; continued medical research into related psychological conditions

sources.author: M. Scott Peck
sources.work: Glimpses of the Devil: A Psychiatrist's Personal Accounts of Possession, Exorcism, and Redemption
sources.title: M. Scott Peck

sources.author: Michael Cuneo
sources.work: American Exorcism: Expelling Demons in the Land of Plenty
sources.title: Michael Cuneo
